CloudSync
Engineering March 15, 2026 · 8 min read

How We Built Sub-Second Sync with Delta Compression

When we set out to build CloudSync, we had one non-negotiable goal: file synchronization should be so fast that users never notice it happening. Today, the average CloudSync file syncs across all connected devices in under 800 milliseconds. Here's how we got there.

The Problem with Traditional Sync

Most file sync solutions work by uploading the entire file every time a change is detected. For a 50MB presentation where you changed a single slide, that means re-uploading all 50MB. Over a slow connection, that can take minutes — and during that time, your team is working with outdated files.

"We realized early on that the bottleneck wasn't network speed — it was the amount of data being transferred. If we could reduce the payload to only what changed, speed would follow."

Delta Compression: The Core Innovation

Our sync engine works by computing a binary diff between the old and new versions of a file. We use a custom implementation of the rsync rolling-checksum algorithm, optimized for modern hardware with SIMD instructions.

Here's what happens when you save a file:

  • The local client detects the change via filesystem events (using fswatch on macOS, inotify on Linux, ReadDirectoryChangesW on Windows)
  • A rolling checksum splits the file into variable-size blocks
  • Only blocks that differ from the server version are uploaded
  • The server reconstructs the full file and pushes deltas to other clients

Real-World Results

For a typical office document (5-50MB), our delta compression reduces the upload payload by 95-99%. A 50MB file with a single-page edit generates a delta of approximately 50-200KB — that uploads in under 100ms on most connections.

Combined with our persistent WebSocket connections and edge-cached metadata, the end-to-end sync time from "save" to "available on another device" averages 780ms globally.

What's Next

We're working on predictive pre-fetching, where the client anticipates which files a user will need based on their recent activity patterns. Early tests show this can make file access feel truly instantaneous.

Elena Petrova

CTO & Co-Founder

Elena leads the engineering team at CloudSync. Previously a distributed systems engineer at Google, she's passionate about making infrastructure invisible.
